era_context: The late 1760s marked a volatile transition in global affairs. Europe's Enlightenment-era monarchies competed for colonial and continental advantage while radical ideas about sovereignty and natural rights circulated in salons, coffeehouses, and clandestine publications. Britain's North American colonies were in escalating conflict over Parliamentary taxation. The Ottoman Empire and Russia were drifting toward war over Poland and the Black Sea. In South and Southeast Asia, the collapse of the Ayutthaya kingdom (1767) and the fragmentation of Mughal authority opened spaces for new state-builders. The Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor remained the world's most powerful state, while the Atlantic slave trade operated at enormous scale.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading states and Ethiopia
Aboriginal Australian societies prior to European contact
Pacific Island communities beyond those contacted by European voyagers
Central Asian steppe polities between Russian and Qing spheres

events.title: Formation of the Bar Confederation in Poland
events.description: On 29 February 1768, Polish-Lithuanian nobles formed the Confederation of Bar in the fortress town of Bar in Podolia, opposing King Stanisław August Poniatowski and Russian interference in Polish affairs. The immediate catalyst was the Russian-imposed guarantee of rights for religious dissidents (the Repnin Sejm of 1767–68), seen as a violation of Catholic noble prerogatives and Polish sovereignty. The confederation launched a multi-year armed uprising that drew in Ottoman, French, and Austrian interests, destabilising the entire region. It ultimately failed militarily by 1772, but its collapse provided the pretext for the First Partition of Poland by Russia, Prussia, and Austria.
key_figures: Kazimierz Pułaski (military commander, later hero of American Revolution)
Michał Krasiński (Bishop of Kamieniec, political leader)

sources.name: Jerzy Łojek, Historia pięknej bitewki: Konfederacja barska (1992); Confederation documents and correspondence
Contemporary confederation acts and manifestos, February–March 1768
Robert Howard Lord, The Second Partition of Poland (1915), covering background of the First Partition

events.certainty_note: Well documented in Polish, Russian, and French diplomatic archives. The formation date is firmly established.

events.title: Ottoman Empire Declares War on Russia
events.description: On 6 October 1768 (25 September Old Style), the Ottoman Empire declared war on Russia, inaugurating the Russo-Turkish War of 1768–1774. The immediate trigger was the violation of Ottoman territory by Russian Cossacks pursuing Bar Confederates into the Ottoman border town of Balta. Deeper causes included Russia's growing dominance over Poland-Lithuania, Ottoman fears about the Black Sea balance of power, and French diplomatic encouragement of Ottoman belligerence. The war would prove catastrophic for the Ottomans, ending with the Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca (1774) that established Russian influence over Crimea and the Black Sea.
key_figures: Comte de Vergennes (French ambassador to Constantinople, encouraged Ottoman action)

sources.name: Ottoman court chronicles (Vak'anüvis records) and declaration of war document
Virginia Aksan, Ottoman Wars 1700–1870: An Empire Besieged (2007)
Brian Davies, The Russo-Turkish War, 1768–1774: Catherine II and the Ottoman Empire (2016)

events.certainty_note: Formal declaration of war is precisely dated in both Ottoman and Russian records.

events.title: Koliyivshchyna Uprising in Right-Bank Ukraine
events.description: In May–June 1768, a massive Cossack and peasant uprising known as the Koliyivshchyna erupted in Right-Bank Ukraine, then under Polish-Lithuanian rule. Led by Maksym Zalizniak and Ivan Gonta, the rebels attacked Polish nobles, Catholic clergy, and Jewish communities. The massacre at Uman in June 1768 killed thousands, including a large portion of the town's Jewish population — one of the deadliest pogroms of the 18th century. The uprising was simultaneously anti-feudal, anti-Polish, and Orthodox-chauvinist. It was ultimately suppressed by combined Russian and Polish forces, as Russia feared the rebellion threatened regional stability.

sources.name: Polish and Russian military reports and correspondence, 1768
Nathan Hanover tradition and Jewish communal chronicles attesting to the Uman massacre
Orest Subtelny, Ukraine: A History (4th ed., 2009)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Polish, Russian, Ukrainian, and Jewish sources. Death tolls are debated but the events themselves are certain.

events.title: British Troops Occupy Boston
events.region: Massachusetts Bay Colony, British North America
events.description: On 1 October 1768, two regiments of British regulars (approximately 2,000 troops) landed in Boston to enforce customs collection and suppress growing colonial resistance to the Townshend Acts. The deployment followed months of tension including the Liberty Affair (seizure of John Hancock's sloop in June) and a circular letter from the Massachusetts legislature urging colonial unity against Parliamentary taxation. The military occupation of a civilian town without local consent radicalised colonial opinion and set the stage for the Boston Massacre of March 1770. It represented a critical escalation from legislative disputes to physical confrontation.
key_figures: Thomas Gage (Commander-in-Chief of British forces in North America)
John Hancock (merchant, whose ship was seized)

sources.name: Boston town meeting records and Massachusetts General Court correspondence, 1768
Hiller Zobel, The Boston Massacre (1970)
Contemporary Boston Gazette accounts, October 1768

events.certainty_note: Precisely dated from military orders, ship logs, and extensive contemporary newspaper coverage.

events.title: France Acquires Corsica from Genoa
events.description: On 15 May 1768, the Treaty of Versailles transferred Genoese sovereignty over Corsica to France. Genoa, unable to suppress the long-running Corsican independence movement led by Pasquale Paoli, effectively sold the island to France in exchange for debt relief. French troops invaded in force and defeated Paoli's forces at the Battle of Ponte Novu in May 1769, completing the conquest. The acquisition had the incidental consequence of making the Buonaparte family French subjects — Napoleon Bonaparte was born in Ajaccio on 15 August 1769, just months after the French takeover became effective.
key_figures: Duc de Choiseul (French Foreign Minister, orchestrated the acquisition)

sources.name: Treaty of Versailles, 15 May 1768 (Corsica cession)
Peter Thrasher, Pasquale Paoli: An Enlightened Hero 1725–1807 (1970)
Correspondance diplomatique française, 1768

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives and the date is unambiguous.

events.title: James Cook Departs on His First Voyage
events.description: On 26 August 1768, Lieutenant James Cook departed Plymouth aboard HMB Endeavour on his first Pacific voyage. The official mission, sponsored by the Royal Society, was to observe the transit of Venus from Tahiti in June 1769 and to search for the hypothetical southern continent (Terra Australis). Cook carried secret Admiralty instructions to explore and claim new territories. The voyage would result in the European mapping of New Zealand, the charting of Australia's east coast, and enormous botanical and natural-history collections gathered by Joseph Banks and Daniel Solander. It fundamentally reshaped European knowledge of the Pacific.
key_figures: James Cook (Lieutenant, Royal Navy, expedition commander)

sources.name: James Cook, Journal of HMS Endeavour, 1768–1771
Admiralty instructions to Cook, 30 July 1768 (secret instructions)
J.C. Beaglehole (ed.), The Journals of Captain James Cook, vol. 1 (1955)

events.certainty_note: Departure date confirmed in Cook's own journal and Admiralty records.

events.title: Prithvi Narayan Shah Conquers the Kathmandu Valley
events.description: On 25 September 1768, Prithvi Narayan Shah of the Gorkha Kingdom captured the city of Kathmandu during the Indra Jatra festival, completing a decades-long campaign to unify the Kathmandu Valley's three Malla kingdoms (Kathmandu, Patan, and Bhaktapur). Patan fell shortly after, and Bhaktapur would follow in 1769. This conquest marks the founding moment of modern Nepal as a unified state. Prithvi Narayan Shah pursued a deliberate policy of excluding European influence and consolidating the diverse hill kingdoms under Gorkha rule, establishing the Shah dynasty that ruled until 2008.
key_figures: Prithvi Narayan Shah (King of Gorkha, unifier of Nepal)
Jaya Prakash Malla (last king of Kathmandu)
Tej Narasimha Malla (last king of Patan)

sources.name: Divyopadesh (Divine Counsel) attributed to Prithvi Narayan Shah
Father Giuseppe da Rovato, account of the Capuchin mission in Nepal
Ludwig Stiller, The Rise of the House of Gorkha (1973)

events.certainty_note: Date is well established from Nepalese chronicles and contemporary European missionary accounts. The connection to the Indra Jatra festival is consistently attested.

events.title: Taksin Reunifies Siam from Thonburi
events.description: Through 1768, the general Taksin (Phraya Tak), who had escaped the Burmese destruction of Ayutthaya in April 1767, consolidated his authority from a new capital at Thonburi across the Chao Phraya River from modern Bangkok. By late 1768, he had defeated rival claimants and expelled remaining Burmese garrisons from central Siam, reunifying the core Thai territories with remarkable speed. Taksin crowned himself king and established the Thonburi Kingdom. His reunification campaign rescued Thai political identity from fragmentation and laid the groundwork for the Chakri dynasty that succeeded him in 1782.
key_figures: Thong Duang (future Rama I, Taksin's leading general)

sources.name: Royal Chronicles of Ayutthaya and Thonburi (Phongsawadan)
B.J. Terwiel, Thailand's Political History: From the 13th Century to Recent Times (2011)
Chinese merchant community records and trade documentation from Thonburi

events.certainty_note: Core narrative is well established, though exact dates within 1767–68 for specific battles vary between Thai chronicle traditions.

events.title: Founding of the Royal Academy of Arts in London
events.description: On 10 December 1768, King George III signed the Instrument of Foundation establishing the Royal Academy of Arts in London, with Sir Joshua Reynolds as its first president. The Academy provided formal art training, annual public exhibitions, and a framework for professionalising the visual arts in Britain. Reynolds's inaugural Discourses on Art articulated neoclassical aesthetic principles that shaped British and European art for generations. The founding represented Britain's effort to rival the French Académie royale de peinture et de sculpture and to elevate the cultural prestige of the nation.
key_figures: Sir Joshua Reynolds (first President of the Royal Academy)
Angelica Kauffman (founding member, one of two women among the original 36 members)

sources.name: Instrument of Foundation, Royal Academy of Arts, 10 December 1768
Sidney Hutchison, The History of the Royal Academy 1768–1986 (1986)

events.certainty_note: The founding instrument survives in the Royal Academy's archives.

events.title: Louis-Antoine de Bougainville Reaches Tahiti
events.description: In April 1768, the French navigator Louis-Antoine de Bougainville reached Tahiti during his circumnavigation of the globe (1766–1769), unaware that Samuel Wallis had visited the island the previous year. Bougainville's enthusiastic description of Tahiti as a paradise of natural abundance and sexual freedom — which he named 'New Cythera' — profoundly influenced Enlightenment thought about the 'noble savage' and the state of nature. The naturalist Philibert Commerson, travelling with the expedition, collected extensive botanical specimens. The voyage also revealed that Commerson's assistant 'Jean Baré' was actually Jeanne Barret, likely the first woman to circumnavigate the globe.
key_figures: Jeanne Barret (botanist's assistant, disguised as male)
Ahutoru (Tahitian who chose to sail to France with Bougainville)

sources.name: Louis-Antoine de Bougainville, Voyage autour du monde (1771)
John Dunmore, French Explorers in the Pacific, vol. 1 (1965)

events.certainty_note: Bougainville's published journal and ship logs provide precise dates.

events.title: Hyder Ali's War with the Marathas and Nizam
events.description: Through 1768, Hyder Ali, the de facto ruler of Mysore, was engaged in a complex war against the combined forces of the Maratha Confederacy and the Nizam of Hyderabad. The Marathas under Madhavrao I had invaded Mysore in 1767, forcing Hyder Ali into a difficult defensive campaign. By early 1768 he was compelled to sue for peace, ceding significant territory and paying a large indemnity to the Marathas. This humiliation drove Hyder Ali to seek alliance with the Nizam and later confrontation with the British East India Company, shaping the power dynamics of southern India for the next three decades.
key_figures: Madhavrao I (Peshwa of the Maratha Confederacy)

sources.name: Maratha Daftar records and diplomatic correspondence
Mohibbul Hasan, History of Tipu Sultan (1951; reprint 2005)
British East India Company dispatches from Madras, 1768

events.certainty_note: Major military events are well documented, though exact dates of specific engagements within this campaigning season vary across Maratha, Mysorean, and British sources.

events.title: Publication of the First Edition of the Encyclopædia Britannica Begins
events.description: In 1768, the first weekly parts of the Encyclopædia Britannica began publication in Edinburgh, edited by William Smellie and printed by Colin Macfarquhar and Andrew Bell. Issued in 100 weekly instalments at sixpence each, the first edition would be completed in 1771 in three volumes. Unlike the French Encyclopédie's alphabetical integration of radical philosophy, the Britannica organised knowledge into longer treatises on major subjects supplemented by shorter dictionary-style entries. It became the most enduring English-language encyclopaedia and a monument of the Scottish Enlightenment.

sources.name: Encyclopædia Britannica, 1st edition (1768–1771), title pages and publication records
Herman Kogan, The Great EB: The Story of the Encyclopædia Britannica (1958)

events.certainty_note: Publication date established from surviving instalments and Edinburgh publishing records.

events.title: Burmese Invasion of Siam — Siege of Chiang Mai
events.region: Northern Siam / Lan Na (modern northern Thailand)
events.description: In 1768, the Konbaung dynasty of Burma (Myanmar) under King Hsinbyushin continued to exert military pressure on mainland Southeast Asia following the destruction of Ayutthaya. Burmese forces maintained control over Lan Na (the Chiang Mai region) and attempted to consolidate their northern Siamese conquests even as Taksin was reasserting Thai control in the central plains. However, Burma's attention was increasingly diverted by four Chinese invasions from Yunnan (the Sino-Burmese War, 1765–69), which forced significant redeployments northward and weakened Burma's hold on its Thai conquests.
key_figures: Maha Thiha Thura (Burmese general in Lan Na)

sources.name: Glass Palace Chronicle (Hmannan Yazawin), Burmese royal chronicle
Victor Lieberman, Strange Parallels: Southeast Asia in Global Context, c. 800–1830, vol. 1 (2003)
Qing dynasty military records related to the Sino-Burmese War

events.certainty_note: The broad strategic situation is well documented, but specific Burmese operations in Lan Na during 1768 are less precisely dated than the Chinese campaigns.

events.title: Massachusetts Circular Letter and Colonial Non-Importation
events.description: In February 1768, the Massachusetts House of Representatives issued a Circular Letter, drafted primarily by Samuel Adams, to the other colonial legislatures, urging coordinated resistance to the Townshend Acts and asserting that taxation without representation violated natural rights. Lord Hillsborough, the new Secretary of State for the Colonies, demanded that Massachusetts rescind the letter and ordered colonial governors to dissolve any legislature that endorsed it. The Massachusetts House voted 92-17 to refuse rescission. This act of coordinated colonial defiance, combined with expanding non-importation agreements among colonial merchants, marked a decisive step toward intercolonial unity against British policy.
key_figures: Samuel Adams (principal author of the Circular Letter)
Lord Hillsborough (Secretary of State for the Colonies)

sources.name: Massachusetts House of Representatives, Circular Letter, 11 February 1768
Hillsborough circular to colonial governors, 21 April 1768
Merrill Jensen, The Founding of a Nation: A History of the American Revolution 1763–1776 (1968)

events.certainty_note: Original documents survive in Massachusetts state archives and British colonial office records.

events.title: Qing Campaign Against Burma Continues
events.description: In 1768, the Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor launched its third major invasion of Burma as part of the Sino-Burmese War (1765–1769). General Mingrui led a large army into Upper Burma but was ambushed and decisively defeated by Burmese forces under Maha Thiha Thura near the Irrawaddy. Mingrui committed suicide rather than face capture. The catastrophic failure humiliated the Qianlong Emperor and prompted an even larger fourth invasion in 1769. The war demonstrated the limits of Qing power projection in mainland Southeast Asia and diverted Burmese resources from consolidating their conquest of Siam.
key_figures: Mingrui (Qing general, committed suicide after defeat)

sources.name: Qing Veritable Records (Qing Shilu), military campaign entries for 1768
Yingcong Dai, The Sichuan Frontier and Tibet: Imperial Strategy in the Early Qing (2009); and broader Qing military historiography
Glass Palace Chronicle (Hmannan Yazawin)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in both Qing and Burmese sources. Mingrui's defeat and death are firmly established.

events.title: Sterne Publishes A Sentimental Journey; Dies Shortly After
events.description: In February 1768, Laurence Sterne published A Sentimental Journey Through France and Italy, a landmark work of sentimental fiction that influenced European literature for decades. Sterne died on 18 March 1768, just weeks after publication, likely of tuberculosis complicated by pleurisy. The novel, with its emphasis on emotional experience and subjective perception over plot and external event, helped define the sentimental literary movement and influenced writers from Goethe to Pushkin. It represented a radical departure from conventional travel writing and narrative fiction alike.

sources.name: Laurence Sterne, A Sentimental Journey Through France and Italy (1768), first edition
Ian Campbell Ross, Laurence Sterne: A Life (2001)

events.certainty_note: Publication date and Sterne's death date are documented in contemporary records and correspondence.

events.title: Ali Bey al-Kabir Consolidates Power in Egypt
events.description: By 1768, Ali Bey al-Kabir, a Mamluk leader, had established effective control over Egypt, marginalising the Ottoman-appointed governor and rival Mamluk factions. He was accumulating military and fiscal power that would lead to his declaration of virtual independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1769–1770. Ali Bey exploited Ottoman distraction with the Russian war to expand his autonomy, eventually invading Syria and briefly controlling the Hejaz. His rise exemplified the devolution of Ottoman provincial authority to local strongmen in the late 18th century.
key_figures: Ali Bey al-Kabir (Mamluk strongman, de facto ruler of Egypt)
Abu al-Dhahab (Ali Bey's lieutenant, later rival)

sources.name: Al-Jabarti, 'Aja'ib al-athar fi al-tarajim wa-l-akhbar (Chronicle of events in Egypt)
Jane Hathaway, The Politics of Households in Ottoman Egypt: The Rise of the Qazdağlıs (1997)

events.certainty_note: Al-Jabarti's chronicle provides detailed near-contemporary coverage. Ali Bey's consolidation of power through 1768 is well attested.

events.title: Samuel Hearne's First Expedition from Hudson's Bay
events.region: Rupert's Land, British North America (modern northern Canada)
events.description: In November 1768, Samuel Hearne departed Prince of Wales Fort (Churchill, Hudson's Bay) on his first attempt to reach the Coppermine River and find the rumoured copper deposits and a Northwest Passage. This first expedition failed within weeks due to inadequate preparation and the loss of supplies. However, it began a series of overland journeys (1769–1772) during which Hearne, guided by Chipewyan Dene leader Matonabbee, became the first European to reach the Arctic Ocean overland from Hudson's Bay. The expeditions also documented Indigenous lifeways of the northern Subarctic.
key_figures: Moses Norton (Governor of Prince of Wales Fort, commissioned the expedition)

sources.name: Samuel Hearne, A Journey from Prince of Wales's Fort in Hudson's Bay to the Northern Ocean (1795)
Hudson's Bay Company post journals, Prince of Wales Fort, 1768

events.certainty_note: Departure date is documented in HBC post records. Hearne's published account was written years later but is corroborated by company archives.

events.title: Gustavus Kalmeter's Swedish Economic Reforms Debate
events.description: In 1768, the Swedish Riksdag experienced intense factional conflict between the 'Hats' and 'Caps' parties over economic policy, currency deflation, and foreign policy alignment. The Caps, who had gained power in 1765, implemented a sharp deflationary policy that caused significant economic hardship, contributing to popular unrest. The political instability of the Age of Liberty (1718–1772) was reaching its crisis point, as neither faction could govern effectively and both increasingly courted foreign patrons — France backing the Hats, and Russia and Britain backing the Caps. This dysfunction would culminate in Gustav III's royal coup of 1772.
key_figures: Adolf Frederick (King of Sweden, largely ceremonial role)
Cap and Hat party leaders in the Riksdag

sources.name: Swedish Riksdag protocols, 1765–1769
Michael Roberts, The Age of Liberty: Sweden 1719–1772 (1986)

events.certainty_note: Riksdag records are well preserved. The economic crisis of the Cap period is thoroughly documented.

events.title: Jesuits Expelled from the Spanish Colonies
events.description: Following Charles III's 1767 decree expelling the Jesuits from all Spanish territories, the actual physical expulsion was carried out through 1768 in distant colonial possessions. Thousands of Jesuits were removed from missions, colleges, and parishes across New Spain, Peru, New Granada, the Río de la Plata, the Philippines, and other territories. The expulsion disrupted education, frontier missions (particularly among indigenous peoples), and established social networks. In some areas it provoked popular uprisings, as communities resisted the removal of Jesuit missionaries. The Society's extensive properties were confiscated by the Crown.
key_figures: Conde de Aranda (President of the Council of Castile, oversaw the expulsion)
José de Gálvez (Visitor-General of New Spain)

sources.name: Royal Pragmatic Sanction of 2 April 1767, and colonial implementation decrees of 1768
Magnus Mörner, The Expulsion of the Jesuits from Latin America (1965)
Jesuit correspondence and letters from exile, 1768

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Spanish colonial archives and Jesuit records. Specific local expulsion dates vary by region.

disconfirming_evidence: Several nuances complicate standard narratives: (1) The Bar Confederation is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward patriotic uprising, but modern Polish historiography (e.g., Jerzy Łojek, Jacek Jędruch) emphasises that many confederates were motivated as much by defence of Catholic noble privilege and anti-dissenter bigotry as by anti-Russian patriotism. The Confederation's anti-Jewish violence and opposition to Orthodox and Protestant civil rights complicate heroic framing. (2) Bougainville's idyllic description of Tahiti has been challenged by Pacific historians (e.g., Nicholas Thomas) who note that his brief visit gave a profoundly distorted picture of Tahitian society, omitting warfare, human sacrifice, and rigid social hierarchy. The 'noble savage' reading was a European projection, not an ethnographic observation. (3) The exact date of Prithvi Narayan Shah's capture of Kathmandu is given as 25 September in most sources but some Nepali calendar conversions yield slightly different dates; the connection to Indra Jatra, while widely repeated, may have become embellished in nationalist historiography. (4) Taksin's reunification timeline is often compressed in popular accounts; the process extended well beyond 1768, with significant campaigns in 1769–1770 to bring southern and northern territories under control. His early consolidation was more contested than nationalist Thai historiography sometimes suggests.
historiographic_note: The year 1768 is richly documented across multiple civilisations. European sources are abundant — diplomatic correspondence, parliamentary records, published literature, and shipping logs survive in quantity. South Asian events are documented through East India Company records, Maratha archives, and Persian-language court histories, though with British colonial bias in much of the accessible historiography. Southeast Asian events rely heavily on later royal chronicles (Thai Phongsawadan, Burmese Glass Palace Chronicle) that carry court biases and were retrospectively edited. Qing records for the Burma campaigns are detailed but present an imperial perspective that minimises defeats. The Ottoman-Egyptian nexus is well served by al-Jabarti's chronicle. Indigenous perspectives from the Americas, Pacific, and sub-Saharan Africa are largely absent from the written record for this year, requiring archaeological and ethnohistorical inference.

graph_edges.note: The Bar Confederation and Russian pursuit of confederates into Ottoman territory directly triggered Ottoman declaration of war
The Koliyivshchyna uprising occurred in the same destabilised Polish-Lithuanian context as the Bar Confederation
The Confederation's failure provided the pretext for the First Partition
The war declared in 1768 ended with this transformative treaty
Military occupation of Boston created the conditions for the 1770 confrontation
The troops were sent in direct response to the colonial defiance expressed in the Circular Letter
French acquisition of Corsica made Napoleon a French subject
Cook's first voyage led to European charting of Australia's east coast
Capture of Kathmandu was followed by subjugation of remaining Malla kingdoms
Taksin's reunification created the state that the Chakri dynasty inherited
Qing-Burma war diverted Burmese forces from consolidating Siamese/Lan Na conquests
Qing pressure on Burma indirectly enabled Taksin's rapid reunification of Siam
Ali Bey exploited Ottoman distraction with the Russian war to consolidate Egyptian autonomy
Spanish colonial expulsions were part of the broader Bourbon campaign leading to papal suppression of the Jesuits
